>>62146711
i dropped out of med school. it's not easy. if you have a brain you're better off to law school or doing a top tier MBA. the training period is considerably less (7 years minimum for MD/residency vs 3 years for law school vs 5 years for work experience + MBA.)

62146724
>>62146718

High paying salary floor with potentially unlimited upside if you invent a medical device or start a chain of clinics etc.

62146766
>>62146724

62146774
1. Good haircut (seriously night and day)
2. Go to the gym 2-3 times per week

hardly any effort involved with enormous gains

62146781
>>62145342
this is my gf but shes got abit of a tummy too, is it over or do i just ignore it?

62146786
>>62146766
Well not everyone can make it thru med school but IF you can the ROI is low risk and high reward.
What else is better?
I can't think of anything better.

62146787
>>62146774

The haircut thing keeps blowing my mind too. For the longest time as a teen I had a simple buzz cut. Didn't even consider hair as important. Sometime in college I decided to get the fade with longer hair on top. Immediate, visceral attention from women that I didn't know existed. Started actually getting laid.

Fast forward to about 2021 or so I decide to grow my hair out into more of a mullet thing for fun. Not really complementary when it would get really long and almost afro like. To me though it didn't look bad in the mirror. Recently decided to go back to the fade and once again there are stares and remarks from women that I didn't know were being held back before lol, some bitch even said I looked fat in my mullet pics from before. So I guess I'm stuck with this.

62146788
>>62146781
Get get pregnant and watch that belly grow!
They get HUGE after 36 weeks.
Whew lad.
You think they won't get too big and then they do.

62146796
>>62145342
Here are some life tips I've learned:

- don't fall for the self-sufficiency wild west man meme. Focus on what skills actually make you money today and now. Better to be some banker who can easily pay someone else to cook and DIY for him when you earn $200 an hour. I wasted WAY too many years trying to do everything myself.

- Most bad habits only take 2 weeks of actual effort to quit. So don't think you have to quit something forever, think about 2 weeks and you'll find that's enough.

- For a potential partner her parents are VERY important. Seriously look the fuck at them. When someone is young and hot you can think it doesnt matter but trust me they will mutate exactly into their parents. You want to see if their parents have hobbies, good health and hopefully managed to raise several kids. Parents to one or two who sit and watch TV? Regard that girl as if she's got fucking HIV.

- Sounds basic but avoid sat fats and sugar where you can. Easy to feel like it's not super important when you are young but the second half of your life will be fucked if you don't. Bits of your brain dying with strokes, heart failure, venous insufficiency etc and none of it can be undone at that point.

62146806
>>62146786
>just wait 10 years before you start making real money bro
Being a doctor is only good if its your passion, otherwise it's a faggot career path that gets mogged by engineering professions like Computer Science. Right out of school, i made 80k. Right now i make $160k in a low cost of living area and i work from home. I work about 2 hours per day and i'm not exagerating.

Computer Science was goated, but now you already need like 6+ years of experience like me because we just use AI now, but you still need to understand what the AI is outputting but we barely do any work.

Oh yeah and you can literally do two programming jobs too which i did before but realized i like free time and lazying around more. Time is way more valuable than more money. More money for what? That being said I'll probably pick up another job again soon, maybe a giga-chill low pay (~120k) second WFH job that barely has any meetings to combine with my current one.

What other path allows you to WFH indefinitely, barely have any meetings, and you get to stay on the computer all day doing interesting work? I'm so happy that i swapped to CS in undergrad, you have no idea. If you like computers and learning about computers all the time and it won't feel like a chore to you, CS is STILL the way to go. I hear it's hard now to get started but i'm sure you can utilize LLMs to help you study rather than fighting against it.

62138680
People with more money than you won't give a shit and people with less money than you will resent you.

Keep your mouth shut and if anyone questions your purchases or your lifestyle, pretend to be in debt.

62138700
>>62138453
Keep that shit private, openly talking about how much money you have casually is trashy. Only discuss money with family with the intention of planning / sharing, otherwise they'll always have the thought that you have more to yourself. Speaking from experience with a good family, money brings out the absolute worst in people.

62131658
It is still cheap right now. Don't buy my bags at 25$. You can buy them now at 1.28$. Don't FOMO in at 50$ guys.

62131731
>>62116681
>What exactly does it have that sets it apart from Solona and ETH?
Solana has a success rate associated with it's transactions (see https://solscan.io/analytics). That's kind of fucked up that there's a 10 to 20% chance your tx might not get processed, right? I've never seen a DOT tx fail. DOT doesn't even measure failed tx https://data.parity.io/parachain-stats?selection-mode=include&frequency=daily&relay-chain=polkadot&chains= because there are none. If you submit a tx it'll complete within like 6s to 30s. The longest a tx takes is when you transfer from one corechain to another. So when you bridge (use a HRMP channel) between corechains. And even that never took longer than like 40s from my personal experience. It's bizarre to me that literally up to 20% of the tx simply fail.
Another thing is that SOL literally has more "vote" transactions than anything else; https://solscan.io/analytics#txs . And vote transactions are LITERALLY validator messages to keep the network running. DOT doesn't count that as a tx at all. 60 to 80% of the tx on SOL are from validators to keep the network running. That's insane to think about that you need more tx to run the network than the users use to execute stuff. People are stupid for thinking that SOL processes 3k TPS. More than half of that is just to keep the network running. It's a literal scam number to be honest. It's just to make the normies believe that the network isn't a scam. The number of transactions is completely fake in a sense.
And again, the funny thing is that 1 core of DOT will be able to handle more TPS than the whole of SOL once either JAM or NOMT hit. Maybe even before that once the 500ms Basti blocks hit. If you combine 500ms blocks + JAM + NOMT one core of DOT will be able to probably do anywhere between 2 to 5x more than SOL as a whole and DOT has LITERALLY 100 cores and this will also probably be scaled to more sooner or later. It'll sooner or later scale to about 300 to 500 cores.

62131777
>>62116681
>>62131731
The 300-500 cores are after the network upgrades to 1000 validators from 600 and after a few more future optimizations. But DOT can then scale further by adding more relay chains. So, currently there is 1 relay chain that connects all the corechains (while they also have direct connections through the HRMP channels) and this relay chain has 600 active validators at any given time. At some point DOT could scale further by having multiple relay chains and separate validators on each relay chain and then having a "relay chain" that connects the relay chains. Sure, this would fragment liquidity in a sense but you could still transfer assets between the relay chains in probably like 30s or so... So in a sense the fragmented liquidity would be a non-problem. DOT is truly scalable while other chains simply are not. DOT covered all the problems because Gavin used to develop ETH and noticed "OMG, this won't work out" and then he left ETH in order to develop DOT. DOT is ETH done right in a sense. ETH is just BTC with smart contracts and DOT is an actual solution to all the problems that plague ETH and all the other smart contract platforms.

62135297
100$ end of bull run

Best regards from Mumbai city (The capital of DOT),
Vishnu Kumar, The leader of the Mumbai Warriors

62137662
Soon we shall reach the nirvana of 75$ my Mumbai brothers. Then we shall eat with silver spoons.

Best regards from Mumbai city (The capital of DOT),
Vishnu Kumar, The leader of the Mumbai Warriors

Best regards from Mumbai city (The capital of DOT),
Vishnu Kumar, The leader of the Mumbai Warriors

My brethren, the time is nigh. We're break out. The wave of consciousness is breaking free from the ocean floor.

Any second now BTC will erupt into a storm that has never been seen before. DOT will soon start it's ascension to the heavens for 75$+. We, the Mumbai warriors, shall eat with silver spoons very soon. The time is coming my warriors. You still have to to buy a big fat bag of DOT before you get priced out.

item.title: Arbitrum revealed itself to be a CEX in chain form — 32 replies — 18.2/hr
item.content:encoded:
Arbitrum revealed itself to be a CEX in chain form

Open thread • Replies: 32 • Updated: 2026-04-23 10:01 UTC

OP

https://x.com/arbitrum/status/2046435443680346189?s=20

They had the power all along to freeze any address's funds and take their crypto. Can't even cope with "not your keys, not your crypto" here.

Early replies (first 3)

62138208
>>62138188
My crypto is on ETH, so its my keys & my crypto

62138253
>>62138188
All L2s are centralized, this isnt news.

62138290
>>62138253
This is a different story in that this L2 has the power to spoof your private key. That's NEVER in the discussion of discussing if an L2 is centralized. When L2 and centralization is discussed, it revolves around the ordering of transactions and who controls the nodes.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62138320
>>62138290
Well youre gay? So yah.

62138408
>>62138188
Every l2 is a centralized scam

62138910
>>62138290
It's not the private keys they control it's the tokens.

62138917
>>62138290
>to spoof your private key
no lol. this is very different

62138931
>>62138910
>>62138917

So what is it then if I run a chain and can take possession of your ETH or whatever coin in your wallet? I know USDC can blacklist wallets, is it this at protocol level?

62138967
>>62138931
>is it this at protocol level?
Yes, they can move ETH since it is the chain's native token. Moving ERC20s would be more difficult. What they can't do is impersonate the address, i.e. they cannot forge signatures and pretend to be the private key owner

62139424
>>62138967
What else are they capable of doing that people should be aware of if the security council comes together to conspire against them? Being able to restrict your wallet and deprive you of your own ETH is a really really big deal.

62139448
>>62139424
No it isn't. it's been known for centuries that L2s were always centralized and now that skellyman denounced them there's no incentive for otherwise

62139494
>>62139448
The discussions of centralization were around node transaction handling. Not being able to control the the very ETH in your wallet. You are conflating things.

62139604
>>62138188
This is why I hold LUNC
the only truly decentralized chain

62139909
>>62138188
> executed a technical approach to move funds
kek, L2s are so worthless. ethereum should have never gone into that direction so heavily. it was obvious that the devs would never give up power within their own rollup.

62140324
anything kikes do is a scam

62140341
>>62140324
Let the goyim deal in the real world

62140513
>>62139424
They can do things that a typical validator set can do. This is like arguing that mining pools can conspire to fork the chain. Can they? Sure. Is there an incentive to do it? Not really.

62140566
>>62140513
>They can do things that a typical validator set can do.

And I want to know what that entails. If some entity mindhacked all the nodes in the BTC network or in the ETH network, they still can't take your crypto. All they can do is censor your transactions. To insert fake transactions and rewrite history, they'd have to go through the network to rewrite each node's history individually to recreate the blockchain. What Arbitrum did is out of the scope of what you'd expect from a validator set. So what can they do?

62140574
>>62140566
>they still can't take your crypto.
That's not correct. Blockchains are not immutable, they are democratic, and anything can change at the protocol level simply by changing the node software. There is an active proposal right now to censor Satoshi's coins, and if/when bitcoin decides to fork into quantum resistance, those coins will be de facto censored.

62140675
>>62138408
Eth is a centralized scam
EVM is a dogshit idea that only got traction because of its ability to be gatekept, controlled, and manipulated.

62141600
>>62140574
yeah but this is not what we're looking at here. they didn't fork the smart contract or anything like that. instead there seems to be a mechanism in place right now that allows the arbitrum conceal of gigafags to move user funds to another address.

62141625
I remember the chainlink schizos and how they believed arbitrum was also chainlink lmfao

62141991
>>62140574
>That's not correct. Blockchains are not immutable, they are democratic, and anything can change at the protocol level simply by changing the node software. There is an active proposal right now to censor Satoshi's coins, and if/when bitcoin decides to fork into quantum resistance, those coins will be de facto censored.

And all the nodes have to consciously upgrade to this new software version. At the very least there's still a democratic consensus at node operator level to accept such a proposal. That's still an acceptable level of decentralization.

>>62141600
>instead there seems to be a mechanism in place right now that allows the arbitrum conceal of gigafags to move user funds to another address.
Exactly. The only people who have access to this feature is the security council who WE have zero control or influence over. These guys are too weak to sing "Niggas in Paris" in front of their own mirror without breaking down due to guilt over patriarchal oppression and the lamentations of the oppressed.

(They're not on the council, but this is example of the folks in the industry)
https://x.com/PatrickAlphaC/status/2012634410764398795?s=20
https://x.com/va_joe/status/2042239851387290022?s=20

Are we to honestly believe that if the FBI comes slamming on the door, they'll be "decentralized" enough to reject an unjust order to move X user's funds?

What do in

62142780
>>62138188
This. BUMP

62142948
>>62141991
>And all the nodes have to consciously upgrade to this new software version. At the very least there's still a democratic consensus at node operator level to accept such a proposal.
Kind of. In practice, only the main hashrate contributors matter, and that is a vanishingly small group. The classic hardline thinking about decentralization just doesn't comport with reality for any blockchain anymore, and likely never will again.

62143434
>>62138290
Are you retarded?

62145045
Man I wish I sold my eth a few months ago.

62146156
>>62138188
going to be a fun time when these security council members have to argue against a government agency or military request to freeze funds
look at how badly Anthropic got shafted

62146505
I don't understand why everyone is shitting on ETH?
If Dumbledore supplied Hogwarts students with Quidditch equipment and a group of Slithering students made their own tournament with modified rules and didn't invite other houses to play how would that be Dumbledore's fault?

62146512
>>62146505
